summary: Gruber reviews the 5th generation iPad Mini, declaring it a deeply satisfying device that makes no technical compromises despite its lower price point. He frames the new Mini and Air as 'iPad Now' versus the Pro as 'iPad Next,' arguing the Mini hits a sweet spot between phone and laptop that fulfills Steve Jobs's original iPad vision. He contends that many complaints about iOS as a tablet OS disappear on the Mini's 7.9-inch screen, where single-app usage feels natural. The review highlights thumb-typing as a surprisingly major advantage over the iPad Pro, which inexplicably dropped split-keyboard support. Gruber concludes that the iPad Mini knows exactly what it is, while the iPad Pros are caught between iOS simplicity and desktop-replacement ambitions.
key_points: The new iPad Mini has flagship specs (A12 chip, Apple Pencil support, True Tone) at $400, making no technical compromises unlike the original Mini
The Mini is perfectly sized for handheld use and thumb-typing, areas where the larger iPad Pro actually fails due to its dropped split-keyboard support
iOS tablet multitasking complaints dissolve on the Mini's screen size, where single-app usage feels natural and correct
The iPad Mini embodies Steve Jobs's original iPad vision — something literally between a phone and laptop — more than any other iPad
The iPad Pros are identity-confused, caught between iOS simplicity and laptop replacement ambitions, while the Mini knows exactly what it is
money_quotes: I think of the iPad Pros as the iPad Nexts, and these new iPad Air and iPad Mini models as the iPad Nows.
It's the physical manifestation of what Steve Jobs in 2010 said the iPad set out to be: something between a phone and a laptop. He was speaking conceptually but the iPad Mini takes it literally.
Really, in a lot of ways, the iPad Mini feels like the one true iPad, and the others are all just blown-up siblings that don't quite know how to take advantage of their larger displays.
But damn, it's a surprisingly close call, simply because I like this size so much.
I honestly don't know how anyone is supposed to type on an iPad Pro while holding it in their hands. It's crazy.

Basically, the iPad Mini knows exactly what it is and the iPad Pros do not — the iPad Pros are lost between the iOS world of conceptual simplicity and the complex world of competing with desktop OSes.
The iPad Mini puts the 'pad' in iPad.
It's in no way a laptop replacement and doesn't aspire to be. It just is what it is, and what it is is great.
key_insight: The iPad Mini succeeds precisely because it embraces its identity as a handheld tablet rather than chasing laptop replacement, making it feel like the truest realization of the original iPad concept.
